title: Making a simple dog lead from leather
subtitle: Cut and assemble a strap lead with a trigger clip and loop handle
excerpt: How to make a sturdy leather dog lead: cutting a strap from hide, skiving and finishing the edges, punching holes for the handle loop and clip attachment, and securing both ends with Chicago screws or rivets.
sourceNotes: Al Stohlman The Art of Hand Sewing Leather. Collins Complete DIY Manual. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

glossaryTerms.definition: A two-part metal fastener used in leatherwork: a threaded post and a slotted cap that screws onto it from the front, joining two or more layers of leather without sewing or riveting. Also called a binding screw or sex bolt.

The shaped terminal of a leather strap, cut to a rounded, pointed, or squared profile. Bevelled and burnished before any hardware is attached so the finished end looks deliberate rather than rough-cut.

content.text: A dog lead needs two things: a handle loop at one end and a clip at the other. The structure is a single strap folded and fastened at each end with
or rivets. Use 2 to 3 mm vegetable-tanned leather for a lead that feels substantial but flexes comfortably. Finish the

Vegetable-tanned leather, 2 to 3 mm thick, cut to a strap 20 mm wide and 1200 mm long (adjust for the desired lead length). A trigger clip with a 20 mm bar. Two or three Chicago screws in brass or nickel. A strap-end punch or leatherwork knife. An edge beveller. A leather hole punch (or revolving punch pliers). A leather burnisher and beeswax or wood gum for edge finishing.
Step 1, cut and shape the strap

Cut the strap to length with a clean straight cut using the leatherwork knife against a steel ruler. Shape the trigger-clip end to a rounded strap end, either using a strap-end punch or by marking a radius with a penny and cutting with the knife. Run the edge beveller along all four long edges and the curved end on both faces. Burnish the strap end and all edges before assembly.

Thread the rounded strap end through the bar of the trigger clip, fold the leather back on itself by 40 mm, and mark two hole positions 10 mm and 25 mm from the fold. Punch the holes with the leather punch through both layers. Insert the Chicago screws from the face side and tighten the cap by hand, then by a further quarter-turn with a flat screwdriver to prevent the cap working loose.

At the opposite end of the strap, cut the end square and bevel the edges. Form a loop of comfortable size (typically 130 mm internal length) by folding the end back, then secure with two Chicago screws in the same way as the clip end. The finished lead should be flexible enough to roll up for storage; if the leather feels stiff, work it by flexing the strap repeatedly in both directions before use.
